_doc: Cross-cutting concepts the book repeatedly invokes. `definedIn` points to the note where the concept gets its primary development. `coveredBy` lists chapters that discuss it. Aliases will drive an auto-alias matcher in a later phase.

concepts.label: Receiver budget
concepts.summary: A human has finite time, effective mental hours, and capacity to absorb new information. The constraint is real but not fixed — education, vocabulary, and accumulated structure expand the *capacity* of each hour, even if the hours themselves are fixed. Want is the gate that decides what the budget gets spent on, which is what makes the medium-shapes-want loop a receiver-budget mechanism rather than just a content one. Ch 11's decompression-on-demand is the first structural softening of the per-claim cost the book has been able to name, conditional on faithful LLM substrate custody. Ch 4 (optionality-vs-access) shows the same constraint reflected in artifact design — the optionality-vs-access curve is the receiver-budget constraint refracted into how artifacts have to be designed for their intended audiences.

concepts.label: Complexity ceiling
concepts.summary: The maximum complexity an idea can carry across a network is bounded by network size and accumulated selection/transport pressure. Bigger network, lower ceiling.

concepts.label: Compressed form
concepts.summary: Low-precondition version of an idea. Cheap for receivers to process, plugs into existing mental structures, easy to re-transmit. Free to share, easy to weaponize.

concepts.label: Complex form
concepts.summary: High-precondition version of an idea. Requires receivers to load preconditions, work through implications, decide whether the new structure fits. Expensive but lossless.

concepts.label: Manipulation surface
concepts.summary: What attackers can exploit. Grows with network size in the pure-transport view; with tunable selection-gate criteria in the full view. Recommendation algorithms are selection engines with adjustable weights — the more dangerous formulation.

concepts.label: Three realities (Harari)
concepts.summary: Objective (independent of observer — atoms, planets, dogs), subjective (in one mind — pains, dreams, qualia), intersubjective (real because enough people agree — currencies, nations, brands, laws, religions). The pipeline operates differently on each: objective claims have a fact-of-the-matter the pipeline either transmits faithfully or distorts (the book's diagnostic vocabulary applies cleanly); subjective claims mostly don't traverse the pipeline; intersubjective realities are partly constituted by the pipeline rather than transmitted through it (so the relevant failure mode is capture of the constitutive process, not truth-loss in transit). Ch 2 makes the distinction explicit to flag where the book's diagnostic vocabulary needs translation between reality types.

concepts.label: Bridge zone
concepts.summary: The space between deep specialists and mass audiences. Where journalism, popularizers, influencers, and AI summarizers live. Where selection pressures are most intense and active reshaping happens.

concepts.label: Echo chambers vs. bubbles (Nguyen)
concepts.summary: Epistemic bubbles are missing-info structures; echo chambers actively discredit outside info. Small networks tend toward chambers, which changes what 'infrastructure for integration' has to do.

concepts.label: Curation layer
concepts.summary: Infrastructure that decouples access from quality assurance — low barriers to enter, robust mechanisms to surface the best work. Wikipedia's editorial apparatus, Stack Overflow reputation. From the democratization-paradox post.

concepts.label: Cost-shifting from producers to consumers
concepts.summary: When barriers to creation fall, the cost of quality assessment doesn't disappear — it transfers from producers (who used to bear it as the price of being published at all) onto consumers, reviewers, and maintainers, who have no more time than before and no ledger entry for the cost. The structural move that lets the platform political economy stay invisible: the platform claims credit for democratizing access while the actual access-to-quality collapses, because the cost landed on someone with no balance sheet to absorb it.

concepts.label: Self-capture vs. external capture
concepts.summary: Two flavors of institutional capture at the platform scale, structurally different. External capture: an adversarial selection-tuner (propaganda operation, state actor, ad-tech firm) learns the platform's gate criteria and crafts variants that win at them — the asymmetric arms race shape. Self-capture: the institution's own business model is the captured selection-tuner — engagement maximization is itself a tuning of the gates against truth, with no external adversary. Self-capture is more stable than external capture because there is no captor to defeat, only an equilibrium to dismantle; the captured state and the functional state are the same state. Per the capture-taxonomy these are the two endpoints of the source axis, and the empirically dominant third case is composite capture — external actors riding the gradient self-capture has already established.

concepts.label: Out-competition of institutional carriers in attention markets
concepts.summary: Platforms do not have to suppress institutional carriers (journals, universities, depth journalism) to hollow them. Free engagement-optimized content has approximately zero marginal cost of attention because users produce it; depth content has real cost-per-impression because the institutional apparatus is paid. The attention market clears at zero price for engagement-optimized noise and infinite price for depth, so receivers — with limited attention and no quality-assessment budget — predictably pick the free option. The mechanism that produces Ch 8's asymmetric institutional collapse without any direct attack on the institutions.

concepts.label: Inverted-U relationship
concepts.summary: Refines the binary 'small vs. large networks' framing: there's an optimum. Small networks have their own internal compression dynamics; very large networks collapse under selection pressure.

concepts.label: Creation cheap / review expensive
concepts.summary: When content generation scales faster than curation infrastructure, noise wins regardless of how many gems are in the flood. LLMs accelerate the asymmetry on both sides.

concepts.label: Preservation vs. training (paired institutional functions)
concepts.summary: What 'bureaucracy' was bundling: preservation keeps the un-compressed form alive somewhere; training re-installs the capacity to decode at that resolution in receivers. Each can be done by many institutional forms (monasteries, journals, universities, common-law courts, version-controlled specs) and each fails differently when starved, captured, or displaced. The pair acts as a pump — preservation holds pressure, training releases it into the network — so either alone fails the integration project.

concepts.label: Survival, not victory (integration institutions)
concepts.summary: The prescriptive reframe at the heart of Ch 12. Institutions that succeed at the integration project are institutions that survive being out-competed for attention and still deliver preserve-and-retrain — they do not need to displace the captured equilibrium, they need to operate alongside it without being eroded by it. Survival-design and victory-design produce different institutions: victory-design tries to clear the attention market by being more engaging (and gets captured by the market's selection pressure); survival-design refuses to compete in the attention market and finds funding outside it. The framing matters because almost every modern attempt at integration infrastructure has been victory-designed and has drifted toward engagement-optimization or starved.

concepts.label: Funding decoupling from attention markets
concepts.summary: The structural counter to Ch 10's out-competition mechanism. Institutional carriers cannot survive if they have to clear a free-engagement market — they have to compete with platforms' zero-marginal-cost attention and lose. The only survivable funding shapes are those whose revenue is not a function of how well the institution clears the attention market: endowments and patient capital, public funding through credibly neutral allocation, member dues and mission-driven subscriptions, public-goods funding mechanisms (retroactive funding, quadratic funding, crypto-economic public-goods primitives). Revenue contracted in a different market than the engagement-optimization noise lets the institution lose the engagement-market clearing entirely without losing capacity to operate. The first of Ch 12's three load-bearing design principles.

concepts.label: Survivable polarization (design for partial trust)
concepts.summary: Design principle for integration institutions: build for institutions that work even when trust is partially eroded, not for institutions that require trust to be complete. The polarization-via-distrust trap from Ch 9 means evidence channels get filtered out by communities that distrust the source — so the integration infrastructure has to do its work across trust boundaries that will not be repaired in advance. Properties: decision provenance auditable by skeptics; reputation systems whose underlying signal survives partial distrust in the aggregator; versatile experts inhabiting the institution rather than presiding over it; multiple independent decision pathways for the same question (route around capture, get Zollman-effect accuracy benefits as a bonus). Wikipedia's edit history, common-law jurisdictional variation, replicated lab results are existing partial implementations.

concepts.label: LLM as capability extender (inside substrate custody)
concepts.summary: The Ch 11 salvation case, structurally specified by Ch 12. Faithful LLMs running with institution-owned substrates — corpus, objective, deployment all governed by the institution rather than by a commercial vendor — function as a capability extender for preserve-and-retrain: decompression-on-demand against the institution's preservation archive for trainees, Socratic instruction at scale, drafting and synthesis that versatile experts then verify. Not a replacement for institutional experts but a tool that makes their work go faster and reach more receivers. Requires open weights, transparent corpus custody, credibly neutral objective specification, and institution-controlled deployment — none of which is the default in the commercial LLM landscape. The integration-friendly LLM configuration is technically realizable, economically marginal, and politically blocked by the same forces that captured the social-media equilibrium.

concepts.label: Work of generations (institutional timescale)
concepts.summary: The closing reframe of Ch 12. Institutions of integration are civilization-scale investments on the timescale of universities, journals, common-law systems, and open-source ecosystems — built and drifted on generational timescales, not platform-launch timescales. The captured equilibrium of the platform business model built itself in two decades; the integration institutions that hold against it will need at least that long to be built, and will need to be funded and staffed and politically protected through the building. The prescription is not a recipe for next quarter — it is a brief for the civilization-scale investment that built the institutions the modern environment has been disinvesting from. Makes the timescale of the prescriptive arc explicit so that institution-builders entering the work know what shape it has to take.

concepts.label: Capture taxonomy (substrate × source × target asymmetry)
concepts.summary: The unified treatment of capture across the book. Capture is one mechanism — tuning a selection-design surface against the desideratum it was supposed to serve — operating across three axes: substrate (which selection-design surface is captured), source (external adversary / institution's own operating logic / composite), and target asymmetry (recovery dynamics determined by the substrate). Two cross-cutting principles: consumer-key captures are harder to recover from than surface captures; training-objective capture is uniquely bad because it self-reinforces across model generations. Composition rule: self-capture creates the gradient external capture rides, so composite capture is the empirically dominant mode.

concepts.label: Consumer-key vs. surface capture
concepts.summary: A cross-cutting principle the capture-taxonomy makes visible. Captures of substrates that install decoding-key machinery in consumers (receiver-training, training-corpus, training-objective) are harder to recover from than captures of substrates that only shape the surface a consumer encounters (gate-criteria, option-space, deployment-configuration, preservation-archive). The first kind damages the consumer; the second damages only the substrate. Generalizes Ch 8's capture-asymmetry between training and preservation into a substrate-level principle that applies wherever an LLM corpus, an LLM objective, or an institutional curriculum is the captured surface.

concepts.label: LLM as collapsed selection-design moment
concepts.summary: An LLM is the first node in the book's information pipeline that owns gate + option-space + content-generator + training-corpus + training-objective + deployment-configuration + pricing-tier, all collapsed into one design moment owned by one party. Where a Ch 10 social-media platform owns two or three selection-design surfaces, an LLM owner owns five-to-seven. The structural concentration is what makes the LLM the limit case for the book's political-economy argument: every concern about ownership of selection design applies with sharper force.
aliases: LLM as new kind of node

concepts.label: Decompression on demand
concepts.summary: An LLM, used as a faithful expansion service, can take a compressed claim or a complex source and re-expand it into a form pre-loaded with the preconditions a receiver needs to decode it. This is the first structural way out of the fixed receiver-budget constraint the book has named: the receiver's budget has not changed, but the amount of complex form they can engage per unit of budget has gone up, because the LLM is paying the decompression cost. Conditional on faithfulness — a hallucinating LLM supplies plausible-but-wrong outer messages, which is worse than no outer message at all.

concepts.label: Compressed form becoming authoritative
concepts.summary: The inverse failure mode of decompression-on-demand: at scale, the LLM-compressed summary of a source substitutes for the source itself in the network's working memory, with the LLM's authority backing the compressed version. The original is technically still there; nobody reads it. The summary is technically not the original; everybody reads it. Compression becomes substitution rather than abstraction — the receiver does not in practice go back and check, because the compressed form arrived with epistemic credentials no prior compression had.

concepts.label: Corpus capture
concepts.summary: Capture of an LLM by selection of the training corpus. The most upstream version of capture in an LLM: choosing what data the model was fitted to (what included, in what proportions, with what filtering) tilts every output regardless of how careful the runtime gate is. A captured corpus produces a captured outer message — every output is decoded by receivers using the very preconditions the corpus installed. Will be unified with related flavors in the forthcoming capture-taxonomy note.

concepts.label: Objective capture
concepts.summary: Capture of an LLM by the training objective (loss function, RLHF reward signal). A captured objective produces outputs that score well on the captured proxy and badly on whatever the actual desideratum was. Uniquely bad in the capture-taxonomy because it self-reinforces across model generations — the model's outputs go on to influence what data is generated downstream (the internet itself), which then feeds the next model's training corpus, baking the captured objective's effects across model generations. The substrate with the worst recovery dynamics in the book.

concepts.label: Deployment capture
concepts.summary: Capture of an LLM at inference time through system prompts, fine-tunes, distillations, refusal policies, and tool-use constraints. The cheapest of the LLM capture surfaces because it changes nothing about the model — same weights, used differently per request — and per the capture-taxonomy a surface capture rather than a consumer-key capture. Most opaque in practice because the configurations are typically not published and can change silently between requests.

concepts.label: Capture asymmetry (training vs. preservation)
concepts.summary: Under institutional capture, the training half does more damage and recovers more slowly than the preservation half. A captured preservation institution holds a tuned version of the complex form — in principle re-discoverable from external evidence and alternative archives. A captured training institution tunes the receivers themselves, and trained perceptual machinery cannot be easily un-trained: subsequent un-captured preservation has to work against a re-tuned readership that decodes incoming evidence through the installed key. The asymmetry decides which half a network has to defend first when the institution is under pressure. Per the capture-taxonomy this is the special case of the more general consumer-key-vs-surface principle.

concepts.label: Differential decay rates (preservation vs. training)
concepts.summary: Preservation institutions degrade slowly — archives and journals can run on inertia for decades with the loss mostly invisible until someone tries to use the form. Training institutions degrade within a generation — every cohort needs to be carried fresh, and once the producers of versatile experts stop producing, supply runs out within twenty or thirty years. Together with the capture asymmetry, this makes training the more time-sensitive function to defend even though preservation is what the institution rhetorically advertises.

concepts.label: Memetic fitness
concepts.summary: The local fitness criteria at each selection gate: emotional valence, identity reinforcement, status, action affordance, novelty, controversy. None coordinated, none particularly interested in truth-preservation.

concepts.label: Transferable preconditions
concepts.summary: Field-general capacities (statistical literacy, critical reading, the disposition to look for evidence) that help receivers engage with many domains. The outline's first-pass answer for what makes a bridge node; refined in bridge-nodes-and-versatile-expertise into the paired skill alongside depth rather than the bridge itself.

concepts.label: Versatile expertise
concepts.summary: Deep specialist expertise paired with metacognitive flexibility — the second-order capacity to recognize structural analogies across domains, suspend one's primary paradigm, and treat one's own frame as one option rather than the substrate of reality. The bridge-node mechanism: depth is what gets a bridge admitted as a peer on either side; flexibility is what lets it see and translate the structural analogy that crosses. Refines the outline's 'transferable preconditions' answer into the paired-skill form.

concepts.label: The curse of expertise
concepts.summary: Four cognitive failure modes of deep specialization without paired metacognitive flexibility: paradigm lock-in (can't entertain other frames), overconfidence in cross-application (over-extends mastery), perceptual filtering (screens out information that doesn't fit), conceptual rigidity (can't reframe a problem). Together these are the cognitive substrate of polarization-via-distrust — same mechanism, vocabulary at the individual level instead of the network level.

concepts.label: Handle-ability
concepts.summary: Proposed alternative to 'complexity' as the real gating variable: how many existing mental models an idea plugs into, how much emotional or identity content it carries, whether it tells the receiver what to do. Selection-side property.

concepts.label: Meme ecosystem competition
concepts.summary: Memes don't propagate in isolation — a meme's infection and decay rates are functions of the other memes already competing for the same attention. Fitness is relative to the current population, not an intrinsic property. (Catalog gap flagged by the epidemiological-virality extraction.)

concepts.label: Neutral drift baseline (Yule process)
concepts.summary: The null model for spread: what propagation looks like with no selection at all — random copying plus rich-get-richer accumulation. Most meme variants follow it. Selection is what you detect as departure from this baseline. (Catalog gap flagged by the info-evolution extraction.)

concepts.label: Replicator ontology
concepts.summary: The assumption that culture comes in discrete, copyable units (memes as replicators, analogous to genes). The load-bearing premise of memetics — and the one its critics attack hardest, arguing cultural transmission is reconstructive, not copy-based. (Catalog gap flagged by the memetics-critique extraction.)

concepts.label: Paradox of virality
concepts.summary: What spreads is not what people endorse. Across the political spectrum, people report they do not want high-arousal negative content to go viral — yet it does. The gap implicates the selection gate (especially algorithmic amplification), not receiver preference. (Catalog gap flagged by the psychology-of-virality extraction.)

concepts.label: Superspreader dynamics
concepts.summary: A small number of high-degree nodes drive a disproportionate share of total spread — a network-structure effect with no offline equivalent at the same scale. Distinct from manipulation surface: it's about who is positioned to amplify, not what can be exploited. (Catalog gap flagged by the psychology-of-virality extraction.)

concepts.label: Algorithm as selection engine
concepts.summary: Recommendation algorithms are not lossy transport — they are selection gates with adjustable weights. They make 'who tunes the gates' a literal, operational question, and turn the selection criteria of a few companies into the selection criteria of everyone. (Catalog gap flagged by the psychology-of-virality extraction.)

concepts.label: Epistemic vigilance (mental immune systems)
concepts.summary: The Sperberian-tradition construct for the cognitive defenses humans have evolved to screen incoming information for plausibility and source reliability — 'mental immune systems' in the popular framing. Mercier extends and refines the term as *open vigilance* (see open-vigilance concept) to mark that the system is paired with openness rather than being a one-sided defense layer over a credulous default. The catalog keeps both entries because earlier book chapters used 'epistemic vigilance' before the Mercier extraction sharpened it.

concepts.label: Open vigilance (Mercier's paired construct)
concepts.summary: Mercier's refinement of Sperber's epistemic-vigilance term. The system is paired: humans are simultaneously *open to* useful information and *vigilant against* unreliable sources, with the two faces evolving hand-in-hand as communication became broader and more powerful. The earlier 'epistemic vigilance' label flattens this into a one-sided defense construct, which is what the catalog had until the Mercier extraction surfaced the paired reading. Two named sub-mechanisms: *plausibility checking* (does the message fit pre-existing beliefs?) and *reasoning* (do supporting arguments resonate?). Calibration is asymmetric: more errors of omission (not trusting when we should) than of commission (trusting when we shouldn't) — vigilance is conservative-leaning, not credulous-leaning.

concepts.label: Reflective vs. intuitive belief (Sperber's split)
concepts.summary: Dan Sperber's distinction between two ways a person can hold a belief. *Intuitive beliefs* are integrated with one's other beliefs and freely drive inferences and action — they are load-bearing. *Reflective beliefs* are held propositionally without integration — verbal, contextual, often shared as identity-signals or coalition-markers, but inert with respect to action. The split underwrites Mercier's reading of the paradox-of-virality: 'most false rumors are held reflectively, for they would have much more serious consequences if they were held intuitively.' Conspiracy theories, witches' confessions, absurd flattery, and political-affiliation claims are typically reflective. Explains the share/endorse gap without needing a strong gate-amplification story — the gap is by design at the cognitive layer.

concepts.label: Justification market (behavior→belief arrow)
concepts.summary: Mercier's reframe of the causal arrow between content consumption and behavior. The naive story (and the book's implicit Part-IV-supply-side framing): content shapes belief, belief drives behavior. Mercier's reversal: behavior is determined upstream (by group identity, in-group commitments, status concerns, material interest); receivers then demand-side select for content that *justifies* the already-decided behavior. The market for false beliefs is therefore downstream of action-already-chosen, and what looks like 'mass persuasion installing beliefs' is more often suppliers of justifications meeting receivers' pre-existing demand. The Trump-supporter finding (corrections accepted, behavior unchanged) is the diagnostic anchor: when corrections land and behavior doesn't move, the belief was never load-bearing for the behavior.

concepts.label: The three-layer message (frame / outer / inner)
concepts.summary: Hofstadter's anatomy of any message (Gödel, Escher, Bach, Ch VI): the frame message ('I am a message, decode me'), the outer message (the decoding mechanism the receiver must already hold — implicit, carried by the medium), and the inner message (the meaning the sender intended). The book adapts it: each pipeline re-encoding can strip a layer independently, and the outer message is what the book calls preconditions.

concepts.label: Network segmentation
concepts.summary: The fracturing of a network into sub-groups along the seams of its binding myth's interpretive latitude. The segments affirm the same myth while decoding it differently, so the network is superficially unified and structurally fragmented at once. Distinct from echo chambers vs. bubbles: this is the process that produces those structures.

concepts.label: Truth-compression regimes (preservation / inversion / orthogonality)
concepts.summary: The three things compression can do to a claim's truth value, set by the gap between the idea's decoding key and the key the receiver supplies: preservation (keys close — a low-resolution true image), inversion (keys opposed — a true claim decoded into a false belief), and orthogonality (the claim is read as an identity flag, its truth value intact but no longer the operative axis). The selection gate decides which regime a compressed variant lands in.

concepts.label: Option space
concepts.summary: The set of forms an idea can take at a given pipeline stage — the menu selection picks from. Transport generates candidate variants; the medium bounds which of them are viable; selection ranks what remains. Selection never enlarges the option space, it only chooses within it, which is why the medium that writes the menu is the higher-leverage and less visible surface.

concepts.label: Technology vs. medium
concepts.summary: Postman's distinction between a technology — the machine or tool itself — and a medium — the social and intellectual environment that grows up around how the technology is used. The book's 'medium' always means the latter: not the device but the environment of capacities, gate-criteria, and cultivated wants it produces. The same technology can host different media, which guards against blaming the hardware for what is really a pattern of use.

concepts.label: Pseudo-context
concepts.summary: Postman's term for a structure invented to give decontextualized information the appearance of a use it does not really have. The information may be true and accurately measured, but it has been stripped of the context that connected it to action; pseudo-context — the news quiz, trivia, conversation-fodder — papers over the gap. Distinct from manufactured content, which never measured anything: pseudo-context is real information made idle.

concepts.label: The Orwell/Huxley axis
concepts.summary: Postman's framing of two opposite failure modes for an information ecosystem. The Orwell mode: control by inflicted pain — censorship, surveillance, information withheld. The Huxley mode: control by inflicted pleasure — truth not concealed but drowned in a sea of engaging irrelevance. The book's diagnosis sits squarely in the Huxley mode: the modern failure is abundance optimized for engagement, not scarcity enforced by a censor.

concepts.label: False consensus among rational agents
concepts.summary: O'Connor & Weatherall's central result: a community of agents who each update on evidence correctly — perfect Bayesians, no irrationality, no gullibility — can still converge on a false belief. False consensus is a property of network structure and the way evidence propagates, not of individual error. It undercuts any diagnosis that locates the problem solely in receivers being careless or stupid.

concepts.label: Credibility weighting
concepts.summary: The practice — rational and unavoidable — of weighting a piece of evidence by how much the receiver trusts its source. In O'Connor & Weatherall's models this is the core mechanism of social belief: no one can verify everything first-hand, so trust in sources does the work. It is also the seam propaganda exploits — manufacture or buy credibility and the weighting carries false evidence as readily as true.

concepts.label: Conformity
concepts.summary: The adoption of a belief because one's peers hold it, rather than on the evidence for it. Distinct from the content-side criteria of memetic fitness: conformity weights the social fact of agreement itself. In O'Connor & Weatherall's models conformity pressure can lock a community onto a belief — true or false — and insulate it from correcting evidence.

concepts.label: Polarization via distrust
concepts.summary: A self-reinforcing loop formalized by O'Connor & Weatherall: when agents discount evidence from sources they distrust, communities that begin with mild disagreement diverge — each side discounts the other's findings, beliefs pull apart, and the divergence deepens the distrust that drove it. Polarization here needs no irrationality and no misinformation; rational trust-weighting plus an initial difference is enough.

concepts.label: The Zollman effect
concepts.summary: The counterintuitive result (Kevin Zollman, central to O'Connor & Weatherall's account) that increasing a community's connectivity can reduce the accuracy of its beliefs: densely connected agents see each other's preliminary, noisy results too quickly and converge prematurely on whichever answer took an early lead. More communication is not monotonically better — there is an optimum. Bears directly on the Part IV integration question.
